content.text: A tool lifecycle, deprecation, and replacement policy defines the stages a tool moves through from proposal to retirement, the triggers that initiate each transition, and the operational steps required at each stage. It treats tools in an AI agent's toolbox as managed software artifacts—subject to versioning, security review cadences, ownership accountability, and controlled end-of-life procedures—rather than static configuration that persists indefinitely.

The policy covers five lifecycle stages: Proposed, Approved, Active, Deprecated, and Retired. Each stage has defined entry criteria, exit criteria, and responsibilities for tool owners, platform teams, and agent developers. The policy also specifies standard timelines for the deprecation window and the conditions under which a tool may be emergency-retired outside the normal schedule.

Agentic AI systems accumulate tool dependencies faster than traditional software. A single agent may invoke fifteen to thirty tools across its workflow. Without lifecycle governance, the tool registry grows monotonically: old tools are never removed, security reviews expire silently, and agents continue calling endpoints that have been abandoned by their vendors or superseded by better alternatives.

Unmanaged tool accumulation creates compounding risk. Retired endpoints fail unpredictably. Unreviewed tools receive no security updates. Developers building new agents inherit assumptions about tool availability that are no longer valid. A formal lifecycle policy converts tool management from an implicit assumption into an explicit, auditable practice.
Deprecated tools are systematically removed before they become a security liability rather than after an incident.
Teams building agents know how much notice they will receive before a tool is retired and what the migration path is.
Each lifecycle transition is documented with a timestamp, trigger reason, and responsible party.
Active pruning of retired tools keeps the registry small, discoverable, and accurate.
Deprecated tools are often replaced by lower-cost alternatives. Formalising replacement ensures teams adopt the cheaper option promptly.
Deprecating a widely-used tool forces every dependent agent team to update their tool configuration. This is real engineering work that must be planned and resourced.
Finding all agents that call a deprecated tool requires runtime telemetry. Teams without call-graph visibility cannot produce a reliable dependency list.
When a vendor announces EOL, the enterprise timeline must compress to match. Internal 90-day deprecation windows may be impossible if the vendor retires the API in 30 days.
A critical CVE or vendor breach may require immediate tool retirement with no deprecation window, forcing rapid migration and potential agent downtime.
The five stages form a linear progression with defined entry and exit criteria at each boundary.

A tool enters the marketplace process. It exists in the approval workflow as a ticket but is not yet callable by any agent. Entry: intake form submitted. Exit: approval decision (approve → Approved, reject → removed from pipeline).

The tool has passed security review but has not yet been integrated into any production agent. It is visible in the registry with an 'available' status. Entry: security review board approval. Exit: first production agent configured to use it (→ Active), or approval expiry without adoption (→ review extension or removal).

The tool is in use by one or more production agents. It undergoes periodic re-review on the schedule defined by its risk tier. Entry: first confirmed production call. Exit: deprecation trigger event (→ Deprecated) or emergency condition (→ Retired directly).

The tool has received a sunset notice. Existing agent integrations continue to function during the deprecation window, but no new agent deployments may add the tool. Entry: deprecation trigger event + notice published. Exit: end of deprecation window (→ Retired) or trigger resolved (→ Active, rare).

The tool is blocked in the agent runtime. Any agent that attempts to invoke a retired tool receives a configuration error at startup or a tool call rejection at runtime. Entry: end of deprecation window. The tool record is retained in the registry with Retired status for audit purposes but never returns to Active.

attrs.alt: Tool lifecycle stages and deprecation timeline diagram

content.text: Unpatched CRITICAL CVE, failed security re-review, vendor breach
Vendor EOL announcement, cost threshold exceeded, contract termination
90 days or vendor notice, whichever is shorter
Replacement tool approved and active, capability no longer required
Regulatory change invalidates tool use, policy update, data residency violation
Persistent SLA breach (>3 months), vendor unresponsiveness
Once a registry exceeds 20-30 tools, informal EOL management breaks down. Formalise the lifecycle before this point.
Any environment subject to SOC 2, HIPAA, or ISO 27001 will face audit questions about how tools are decommissioned and access removed.
When multiple teams build agents independently, a central lifecycle policy prevents teams from holding onto deprecated tools indefinitely.
When a key tool vendor announces end-of-life, the lifecycle policy provides the governance framework for managing the transition.

Build lifecycle management into the tool registry from the start rather than retrofitting it. The registry should expose lifecycle status as a machine-readable field that agent runtimes can query. Agent deployment pipelines should fail if any configured tool is in Deprecated or Retired status.
Add a lifecycle_status field to every tool registry entry with values: proposed, approved, active, deprecated, retired.
Add a deprecation_date field set when the tool enters Deprecated status, and a retirement_date field set when it enters Retired.
Build an automated notifier that emails tool owners and all dependent agent team contacts when a tool's status changes.
Integrate registry queries into agent deployment CI pipelines: if any tool in the agent's config is deprecated or retired, fail the deployment with a clear error message.
Build a dependency graph from runtime telemetry: track which agent IDs call which tool IDs so deprecation notices reach the right teams.
Define the migration path in the deprecation notice: link to the replacement tool's registry entry and provide a migration guide.

from dataclasses import dataclass, field
from enum import Enum
from datetime import date, timedelta
from typing import Optional
import smtplib, json
from pathlib import Path

class LifecycleStatus(str, Enum):
    PROPOSED = "proposed"
    APPROVED = "approved"
    ACTIVE = "active"
    DEPRECATED = "deprecated"
    RETIRED = "retired"

@dataclass
class ToolRegistryEntry:
    tool_id: str
    title: str
    version: str
    status: LifecycleStatus = LifecycleStatus.PROPOSED
    owner_email: str = ""
    dependent_agent_teams: list[str] = field(default_factory=list)
    approval_date: Optional[date] = None
    deprecation_date: Optional[date] = None
    retirement_date: Optional[date] = None
    replacement_tool_id: Optional[str] = None
    deprecation_reason: str = ""

    def deprecate(
        self,
        reason: str,
        notice_days: int,
        replacement_id: Optional[str] = None,
    ) -> None:
        if self.status != LifecycleStatus.ACTIVE:
            raise ValueError(f"Cannot deprecate a tool in {self.status} status")
        self.status = LifecycleStatus.DEPRECATED
        self.deprecation_date = date.today()
        self.retirement_date = date.today() + timedelta(days=notice_days)
        self.deprecation_reason = reason
        self.replacement_tool_id = replacement_id

    def retire(self) -> None:
        if self.status != LifecycleStatus.DEPRECATED:
            raise ValueError("Can only retire a deprecated tool")
        if date.today() < self.retirement_date:
            raise ValueError(
                f"Retirement date {self.retirement_date} has not been reached"
            )
        self.status = LifecycleStatus.RETIRED

    @property
    def is_callable(self) -> bool:
        """Returns True only if agents may invoke this tool."""
        return self.status in (LifecycleStatus.APPROVED, LifecycleStatus.ACTIVE)

    def days_until_retirement(self) -> Optional[int]:
        if self.retirement_date is None:
            return None
        return (self.retirement_date - date.today()).days

def check_agent_tool_config(agent_tool_ids: list[str], registry: dict[str, ToolRegistryEntry]) -> None:
    """Fail deployment if agent references any non-callable tool."""
    errors = []
    for tool_id in agent_tool_ids:
        entry = registry.get(tool_id)
        if entry is None:
            errors.append(f"Tool {tool_id} not found in registry")
        elif not entry.is_callable:
            errors.append(
                f"Tool {tool_id} is {entry.status}: "
                f"retirement date {entry.retirement_date}, "
                f"replacement: {entry.replacement_tool_id}"
            )
    if errors:
        raise RuntimeError("Agent deployment blocked:\n" + "\n".join(errors))

# Example usage
registry: dict[str, ToolRegistryEntry] = {}

old_tool = ToolRegistryEntry(
    tool_id="legacy-search-v1",
    title="Legacy Search API v1",
    version="1.0.0",
    status=LifecycleStatus.ACTIVE,
    owner_email="platform@company.com",
    dependent_agent_teams=["customer-support", "sales-ops"],
)
registry["legacy-search-v1"] = old_tool

old_tool.deprecate(
    reason="Replaced by semantic-search-v2 with 40% lower latency",
    notice_days=90,
    replacement_id="semantic-search-v2",
)
print(f"Status: {old_tool.status}, Retires: {old_tool.retirement_date}")
print(f"Days until retirement: {old_tool.days_until_retirement()}")

A financial services platform manages a tool registry of 47 tools used across 30 production agents. The platform team runs a weekly scheduled job that checks every tool's review expiry date and sends automated Slack notifications to tool owners 30 days and 7 days before expiry. When a vendor for a widely-used document extraction tool announced a 60-day EOL, the platform team published a deprecation notice the same day, set the retirement date to match the vendor deadline, and linked to an internal replacement tool already in the registry. The dependency graph from runtime telemetry identified eight agents that called the deprecated tool. Each team lead received an automated Jira ticket with the migration checklist and a link to the replacement tool's documentation. Six of the eight teams migrated within 30 days. The remaining two received escalations from their engineering managers in the final two weeks.
Sending deprecation notices without knowing which teams depend on the tool results in missed migrations and production failures at retirement.
Setting a deprecation notice without a firm retirement date allows teams to delay migration indefinitely.
Retiring a tool without a ready replacement forces agent teams to choose between breaking changes and security risk.
If retired tools are only blocked in the registry but not at the agent runtime, agents that bypass the registry can continue calling them.
Discovering a critical vulnerability with no clear emergency retirement protocol causes paralysis when speed matters most.
The replacement tool should be Active in the registry before the deprecation notice is issued, so teams can migrate immediately.
Include a migration guide in the notice:
Every deprecation notice should link to a concrete migration guide: what changes in the tool interface, what configuration updates are needed, and how to test the replacement.
Block Deprecated tools from new deployments:
The CI pipeline check is the most important enforcement mechanism. Do not rely on teams reading notices.
Keep retired records in the registry:
Retain Retired tool entries with their full history for audit and postmortem purposes. Never delete them.
Emergency retirement for a CRITICAL CVE should complete within 24 to 48 hours. Define this SLA in the policy and rehearse it.

As the tool registry grows, lifecycle management automation becomes essential. A registry of 100+ tools requires automated dependency graph analysis, expiry monitoring, and migration progress tracking—none of which is feasible manually.
Track how many dependent agents have migrated from each deprecated tool in real time.
Integrate retirement date enforcement directly into the CI pipeline that deploys agent configurations, rather than relying on developer awareness.
Build the dependency graph from runtime tool call telemetry (which agent called which tool, when) rather than from static configuration files, which may be out of date.
Supports lifecycle status fields on custom entity kinds; built-in dependency graph visualization.
Enforce tool lifecycle status checks as policies at agent runtime, blocking calls to deprecated or retired tools.
Instrument tool call telemetry to build real-time dependency graphs and call frequency dashboards.
Model deprecation campaigns as tracked epics with per-team migration tasks, providing visible progress tracking.

The most critical security aspect of lifecycle management is the gap between deprecation notice and runtime enforcement. A tool that appears in the registry as Deprecated but is still callable creates a false sense of control. Runtime enforcement—through OPA policies, agent startup validation, or tool proxy middleware—closes this gap. Test the enforcement by staging a tool retirement in a non-production environment and verifying that agents fail to start or receive clear errors on tool invocation.

Reliability risk centres on tools that are retired before migration is complete. Monitor agent error rates immediately after each retirement and have a rollback procedure defined—either re-activating the retired tool temporarily or routing calls to the replacement with a compatibility shim.
A formal tool lifecycle policy converts tool management from an implicit assumption into a documented, auditable practice.
Five stages—Proposed, Approved, Active, Deprecated, Retired—each have defined entry and exit criteria and responsible parties.
Dependency graph telemetry is a prerequisite for effective deprecation; you cannot notify the right teams without knowing who calls what.
CI pipeline enforcement that blocks deployments using deprecated tools is more reliable than relying on teams reading deprecation notices.
Emergency retirement procedures must be defined and rehearsed before they are needed, not designed under incident pressure.
